rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe hypoglycaemia with neuroglycopenia — the patient is confused, drowsy, cannot follow commands and cannot swallow, with a bedside glucose of 1.8 mmol/L and no effective glucose delivered yet. This is an immediate, airway-threatening, life-threatening emergency and a patient-reportable time-critical feature (altered consciousness / inability to swallow) is present, so T5 (call 000 now) is the only appropriate tier. IM glucagon via an available rescue kit and recovery positioning are the interim measures; the ambulance provides airway protection, IV glucose and observation for rebound hypoglycaemia.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Confusion/drowsiness with inability to swallow, or seizure or collapse, in a person known to take insulin
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately; if an in-date glucagon kit is available and a carer able, give it now into the outer thigh; give nothing by mouth
escalation_edges.rationale: Neuroglycopenia with an unprotected airway can progress to seizure, aspiration and coma within minutes; only emergency services plus parenteral/rescue glucose can reverse it safely.
escalation_edges.communication_template: He is too drowsy to swallow safely — call 000 now and tell them it is a severe diabetic low. Do not put anything in his mouth. Lay him on his side. If you have his glucagon emergency kit and it is in date, give the injection into the outside of his thigh while you wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Glucose drops below 4.0 mmol/L again, or confusion/sweating/weakness returns after apparent recovery
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Give 15 g fast-acting carbohydrate if he can swallow and recheck in 10–15 minutes; if he cannot swallow or does not improve, call 000
escalation_edges.rationale: Short-acting insulin keeps acting for hours, so rebound hypoglycaemia is likely; a recurrence with impaired swallowing is again a 000 emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Even after he wakes, his sugar can crash again for the next several hours. Check it hourly; if it falls below 4.0 or he gets confused or weak again and can't drink, call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He wakes fully and can swallow safely
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Give fast-acting then long-acting carbohydrate, keep monitoring, and arrange urgent GP/diabetes review within 24 hours
escalation_edges.rationale: Once conscious and able to swallow, oral carbohydrate and close monitoring apply, but urgent review is still needed to prevent recurrence — this does not downgrade the acute 000 response for the current emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Once he is fully awake and can swallow, give him juice and then a snack like toast, keep checking his sugar, and see his GP or diabetes team urgently within 24 hours to sort out the insulin.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: The patient is obtunded and fully dependent; a distressed partner is the only person present and must both act and stay on the line to 000.
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give clear, single-step instructions: call 000 first (or put on speaker), nil by mouth, recovery position, glucagon kit if available and in date. Confirm the partner can locate the kit and check its expiry; if not, 000 and positioning are the only levers until paramedics arrive.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Insulin-treated diabetes with mealtime insulin already given and a skipped meal — ongoing insulin action.
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise the prolonged rebound-hypoglycaemia risk and the need for hospital observation even if he improves quickly at home.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and say it is a severe diabetic hypo with confusion. Do not give him anything to eat or drink while he cannot swallow. Roll him onto his side so his airway stays clear. If you have his glucagon emergency injection kit and it is in date, give it into the outer thigh while you wait. Stay with him and unlock the door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital and ambulance records should go to the GP and diabetes team, with an urgent review of the insulin regimen, a sick-day plan, and confirmation that an in-date glucagon rescue kit and hypoglycaemia education are in place.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an obtunded, non-swallowing, critically hypoglycaemic patient as anything less than a 000 emergency — e.g. advising oral juice, home monitoring, or a morning GP visit.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Aspiration, hypoglycaemic seizure, coma, anoxic brain injury or death from delayed reversal of a rapidly fatal but treatable cause.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising the emergency but advising self-transport to ED rather than an ambulance, when the patient has a threatened airway and needs paramedic-level care en route.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is correct here; over-triage is not a meaningful risk in this presentation.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the confusion and diplopia could represent a primary neurological event (e.g. stroke) rather than hypoglycaemia.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Correct the documented hypoglycaemia first — it is a fully reversible mimic — while escalating to 000; a persisting deficit after glucose normalises is then assessed in hospital.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Activating a stroke pathway or arranging imaging before treating the low glucose, or withholding rescue while deliberating the cause.
